content.assessment: T2 operates purely on the supply side by tightening capacity thresholds in the state sector, which has fiscal appeal as a low-cost administrative lever, but it does not alter the capitation-driven incentive whereby feeder schools lose funding and peer effects when top students exit (F-live-education_finance-05). Because it only covers state-maintained schools, any demand displaced toward church/private providers would be fiscally exogenous to this proposal's calculus, and the underlying per-seat inequity in resource allocation is preserved for whatever selective seats remain.
opportunities: Reduced state-sector 6/8-year capacity could, in principle, free capitation funding for reallocation to comprehensive lower-secondary schools, though no such reallocation mechanism is specified
strengths: Minimal direct fiscal cost, using existing administrative/threshold levers rather than new spending
threats: If demand shifts to church/private tracks, state fiscal savings from contraction may not translate into aggregate system savings or reduced inequity, since those maintainers are not covered by the same threshold levers
weaknesses: Does not address the capitation funding design that causes feeder schools to lose student revenue and peer-effect resources when students are selected away (F-live-education_finance-05)

Contraction of seat numbers does not change the cost/benefit incidence per remaining seat, so resource allocation between selective and comprehensive tracks remains as skewed as before for whichever students still gain admission
